rank_note: Rank intentionally null. site/src/data/indexes/us-states.json contains only 21 of 51 jurisdictions; ranks 9-38 were lost in the original legacy-HTML extraction and the surviving entries were renumbered contiguously, so the displayed rank column is not a national rank. Utah must not be assigned a rank until the index is rebuilt with all 51 jurisdictions.
calibration_note: Not calibrated to the published us-states rank order, which is known to be corrupt. Calibrated to the methodology anchors and to the 17 states assessed at full depth under this methodology: Georgia 26.9, Iowa 30.6, Arizona 36.2, Ohio 38.1, North Carolina 45.0, Alaska 47.5, Oregon 50.6, Colorado 52.5, Wisconsin 53.1, Michigan 53.8, Delaware 53.8, Pennsylvania 54.4, Virginia 55.6, Maryland 55.6, New York 56.9, Illinois 60.0, New Jersey 60.6. Utah at 42.5 sits between Ohio and North Carolina. Composite verified by running computeCompositeFromDimensions from site/scripts/lib/scoring.mjs (methodology v1.2). Verification output: {"composite":42.5,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Inputs AWR 3.4, EMP 2.4, ACT 2.8, EQU 2.6, BND 2.2, ACC 2.6, SYS 3.4, INT 2.2. All 8 of 8 dimensions fall below 4.0, so weaknessFactor is 0 and integrationPremium is 0.0. Four sensitivity cases were also run through computeCompositeFromDimensions rather than computed by hand. Scoring AB3 on the merits at 4 with no cap gives ACC 2.8 and returns {"composite":43.1,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Applying the AB3 floor of 2 instead of the cap gives ACC 2.4 and returns {"composite":41.9,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Raising INT from 2.2 to 3.0, which would require Utah to stop overriding its own ballot initiatives and to respond to state-commissioned evidence, returns {"composite":45,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. Adding 0.5 to every dimension returns {"composite":55,"band":"Functional","integrationPremium":0}. The band is Functional in every case. The composite of 42.5 sits 2.5 points above the 40-point Developing/Functional boundary, outside the 1.0-point instability window, so the band is not flagged as unstable.
attribution_note: Scores Utah state conduct only. Utah was under unified Republican control of both political branches for the entire 2021-2026 window (Governor Spencer Cox from January 2021, Republican supermajorities in both chambers), so no divided-government attribution split is needed. Federal policy imposed on Utah is excluded. Three attribution decisions are load-bearing. First, and central to this assessment, Utah has an unusually large private welfare system operated by the Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints, and Utah has excellent social outcomes including the fourth-lowest poverty rate in the United States. Neither is scored as state conduct. What IS scored is the memorandum of understanding, obtained and published by ProPublica on 2021-12-02, under which the state counts church volunteer hours toward its own federal maintenance-of-effort obligation, allowing the Legislature to avoid at least 75 million dollars in spending it would otherwise have been legally required to make. The private caring is civil society; the accounting decision is state conduct. Second, the shrinking of Bears Ears and Grand Staircase-Escalante national monuments by presidential proclamation is federal action and is not scored against Utah; what is scored is the discretionary decision by Utah to litigate against monuments designated at the request of five tribal nations. Third, the 2018 ballot initiative overrides (Proposition 3 Medicaid via SB 96 in 2019, Proposition 4 redistricting via SB 200 in 2020) predate the 2021-2026 window but are scored under I1 because the redistricting override remained live state conduct throughout the window, culminating in a district court striking the 2021 congressional map on 2025-08-29 and a court imposing a remedial map on 2025-11-10. Federal 287(g) immigration agreements signed by seven county sheriffs are county conduct and are not scored; the agreement signed by the Utah Department of Corrections is state conduct and is noted under E3.

cross_state_consistency_flag.limb_a: Met. S.B. 277, signed by Governor Cox in the 2025 general session and effective 2025-05-07, dissolved the independent seven-member State Records Committee that had heard GRAMA appeals since 1992 and replaced it with a single Government Records Office director appointed by the governor and confirmed by the Senate, and stripped the balancing test from the records statute. This is curtailment of the oversight body itself: removal of its independence protections and narrowing of its remit. It is the Indiana refinement fact pattern, not the Idaho enumerated-exemptions pattern. The Utah Legislature separately received the Society of Professional Journalists Black Hole award on 2025-03-21 for its own records practices.
cross_state_consistency_flag.limb_b: Met. The Office of the Legislative Auditor General and the elected State Auditor continue to publish damaging, uncompelled findings. Three in the past nine months: the performance audit of the Division of Child and Family Services (January 2026, finding more than 3,200 missed 24-hour child safety deadlines), the audit of mental health care at the Utah State Prison (2025-11-19), and the Department of Health and Human Services privacy audit (February 2026). The Massachusetts output contingency is satisfied; the watchdogs are producing, not dormant.
cross_state_consistency_flag.effect: AB3 capped at 3 rather than floored at 2. On the merits alone, given the unusual volume and public availability of OLAG output, AB3 would have scored 4.
cross_state_consistency_flag.scoring_effect: AB3 = 3 gives ACC 2.6 and composite 42.5 (Functional). AB3 = 4 on the merits with no cap gives ACC 2.8 and composite 43.1 (Functional). AB3 = 2 under the floor gives ACC 2.4 and composite 41.9 (Functional). The band is Functional in all three cases.
cross_state_consistency_flag.precedent: Utah joins Virginia, Massachusetts, Washington, Missouri and Indiana as an applied case. It is distinguishable from Idaho and North Dakota, where the precondition was not met, because Utah attacked the oversight body itself rather than adding subject-matter carve-outs or tolerating procedural weakness.

key_evidence: Utah counts Church of Jesus Christ of Latter-day Saints volunteer hours toward its own federal anti-poverty spending obligation under a memorandum of understanding obtained by ProPublica. Over roughly a decade this let the Legislature avoid at least 75 million dollars it would otherwise have been legally required to spend. The low poverty rate in Utah is largely a civil-society and demographic outcome, not a state institutional achievement, and is not credited as such in this assessment.

Utah commissioned its own systematic review of gender-affirming care for minors. The review found the care largely produces positive outcomes and reduces the likelihood of suicide. The Legislature set the finding aside and the House voted on 2026-02-05 to convert the moratorium into a permanent ban. This single act drives E4, AC3, AB2 and I4.

S.B. 277, effective 2025-05-07, dissolved the independent seven-member State Records Committee of Utah, in place since 1992, and replaced it with a single governor-appointed director, while stripping the balancing test from GRAMA. AB3 cross-state rule v3 applied, capping AB3 at 3.

The Office of the Legislative Auditor General found in January 2026 that the Division of Child and Family Services missed 13.7 percent of statewide deadlines in fiscal 2025, including more than 3,200 failures to see an endangered child within 24 hours. The Deputy Auditor General attributed this to an organizational culture in which cutting corners is acceptable.

A November 2025 audit found 26 acutely suicidal people in the Utah State Prison psychiatric infirmary were monitored only at 15-minute intervals in breach of policy, that one suicide went undiscovered for over an hour, that the Division of Correctional Health Services does not collect suicide-attempt data, and that the prison went more than a year without a psychiatrist after July 2024.

Utah has appropriated more than 300 million dollars for Great Salt Lake conservation since 2022 and delivered nearly 400,000 acre-feet of water to the lake between 2021 and 2025 across four consecutive legislative sessions. The lake nonetheless sat at 4,191 feet in June 2026, only three feet above its November 2022 record low.

Utah reported a 91 percent decline in chronic homelessness over ten years. Its own Legislative Auditor General found the figure erroneous, produced by a counting method that changed twice, with the two changes corresponding to the two largest single-year drops.

Protect Utah Workers gathered more than 320,000 signatures against the 2025 public-sector collective bargaining ban, a Utah record and more than twice the 140,748 required. The Legislature repealed HB 267 on 2025-12-09 rather than face the referendum. This is the strongest evidence in the assessment that the institutions of Utah remain movable.

H.B. 102 (2023) extends in-state tuition to refugees, asylees, humanitarian parolees, SIV holders, TPS holders and asylum seekers from the day they arrive in Utah, described by the International Rescue Committee as the most inclusive such law in the country. Governor Cox was one of only two Republican governors to sign a letter urging permanent residency for Afghan evacuees.
The Wasatch Front came off the federal fine-particulate non-attainment list in November 2025 for the first time in 15 years, a measured environmental health outcome.

evidence.quote: There was an overall decline of 18% across all age groups in 2024... the number of adults experiencing IGP dropped from 75,078 to 64,439, young adults decreased from 17,307 to 10,163, and children decreased from 72,814 to 61,151
case workers failed to meet the state's standard of getting eyes on the child within 24 hours more than 3,200 times
